nativeRange.habitat: Flowing, rocky shallows of the lower Rio Tapajós, Pará state, Brazil (Santarém to Pimental, vicinity of Itaituba); shelters in rock crevices in clear, well-oxygenated channels

decorPreferences: numerous snug-fitting caves (ceramic pleco caves, clay tubes, stacked slate) — at least one per fish
smooth rounded river rock and cobbles arranged to create crevices
fine sand or smooth fine gravel substrate
strong directional flow from powerheads or oversized return pump (10–15× turnover)
high dissolved oxygen — surface agitation or airstone supplementation
subdued lighting or shaded zones; driftwood for additional cover

diet.notes: The Queen Arabesque Pleco is a carnivore-leaning omnivore that generally prefers aquatic invertebrates and meaty foods over algae or vegetable matter — a trait shared across the Hypancistrus genus. Sinking carnivore pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis, daphnia, and gammarus form the core of a practical diet; blanched vegetables such as zucchini or pumpkin are accepted occasionally but plant matter tends to make up only a modest portion of what the fish actually eats. Because the species is most active after dark, feeding after lights-out gives it the best access to food before faster tankmates consume it.

goodWith: Apistogramma and other small dwarf cichlids that respect cave territories
avoidWith: other Hypancistrus species (hybridisation risk in the same tank)
large or aggressive cichlids that may harass cave dwellers
fast, boisterous tankmates that out-compete plecos for food
fin-nipping barbs that bother slow-moving bottom dwellers
any predator large enough to take an adult pleco

summary: The Queen Arabesque Pleco (Hypancistrus sp. L260) is a small, distinctively patterned catfish from the rocky fast-flowing channels of the Rio Tapajós in Brazil. Its cream body is covered in fine black maze-like lines. Cave-dependent and nocturnal, it leans toward meaty foods rather than algae and is regularly bred in captivity.
careNotes:
The Queen Arabesque Pleco (L260) is among the more accessible Hypancistrus to keep, but still has the genus's core needs: warm water (generally advised 25–29°C, with 27–28°C a common sweet spot), strong flow, very high dissolved oxygen, and immaculate water quality. Filtration turnover of 10–15× tank volume per hour with supplemental powerheads aimed along the substrate is widely recommended. Weekly water changes of 20–30% are commonly cited.

Like most Hypancistrus, this is not an algae eater. Sinking carnivore pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, gammarus, mysis, and home-made gelatin diets form the practical menu; vegetables are accepted occasionally but should not be the diet's backbone. Feed after lights-out and remove uneaten food promptly.

Provide one snug-fitting cave per fish — the fish should be able to barely turn around inside. Males defend caves and may show mild conspecific aggression around spawning; ample cave count plus visual breaks reduces conflict. Do not co-house with other Hypancistrus species: hybridisation is a documented concern.

Reports vary on minimum tank size (60 L on the small end, up to ~110 L for a small group). A 90 cm-footprint tank of around 100 L is a sensible practical floor for a single fish or small group, accommodating high flow and multiple caves. The species has been established in the hobby since the 1990s and is regularly captive-bred, with both tank-bred and wild-caught specimens available in the trade.
breedingNotes:
Cave-spawner with paternal brood care; regularly bred in aquaria and considered moderate in difficulty for an experienced keeper. Sexually mature at roughly 2–2.5 years. The male occupies and defends a snug cave; the receptive female enters to spawn. Reported clutch size is typically 15–20 yellow eggs, with incubation around 5–7 days followed by another 5–7 days in the cave absorbing the yolk sac. The male fans and guards the eggs throughout.

Conditioning with a high-protein varied diet (bloodworms, mysis, gammarus, quality pellets), maintaining temperatures toward the upper end of the recommended range (~28–29°C), and performing slightly cooler water changes to simulate a seasonal rainfall pulse are commonly cited spawning triggers. Raise fry in a separate grow-out tank on micro-worms, newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, and finely crushed high-protein pellets, maintaining the same high-flow, well-oxygenated conditions as the adult tank.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hypancistrus sp. L260
primary.notes: Used as primary because the designated PlanetCatfish page returned HTTP 403. SeriouslyFish provided taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (lower Rio Tapajós, Pará, Brazil), adult size (80–90 mm SL), water parameters (24–30°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 18–268 ppm), tank footprint (90×30 cm base), diet (omnivore with invertebrate preference), and breeding (cave-spawner, male brood care).

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Queen Arabesque L260 Pleco Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (males ~10 cm, females ~9 cm), temperature (26–30°C), pH (6.5–7.2), hardness (1–12 gH), lifespan (10+, with 15+ reports), diet classification (omnivore, not strictly carnivore), and breeding details (clutch ~15–20 yellow eggs, 5–6 day incubation, sexual maturity 2–2.5 years). Noted minimum tank suggestion of 60 L for 1M+3–4F.

additional.name: Aqua Imports — L260 Queen Arabesque Pleco
additional.notes: Trade source. Confirmed Rio Tapajós (vicinity of Itaituba) origin, water parameters (pH 6.5–7.5, 78–84°F ≈ 25.5–29°C), 3–4 inch adult size (7.6–10 cm), 30 US gal (~113 L) tank suggestion, omnivorous diet with protein bias, and decades-long hobby popularity. Used to triangulate larger-tank recommendation against SF's smaller footprint.

fish.breedingNotes: The Queen Arabesque Pleco is a cave-spawner with paternal egg care. Males defend a snug cave and fan a clutch of roughly 15–20 yellow eggs for approximately 5–7 days until hatching. The species typically reaches sexual maturity at around 2–2.5 years of age. High-protein conditioning and slightly cooler water changes to mimic a seasonal rainfall pulse are commonly cited as spawning triggers.
